project.name: JobSync
project.description: JobSync is a self-hosted, open-source job search assistant (Next.js + Prisma/SQLite, Docker-deployable) for tracking and organizing job applications, with resume AI review/matching, task and activity management, and EURES/ESCO European job discovery. Note: this project has over 100 source files; consider scoping analysis to a subdirectory for faster results.

tour.title: Project Overview
tour.description: Start with the README to understand what JobSync is: a free, open-source, self-hosted job-search companion that runs as a Docker container on localhost:3737. It tracks applications, manages resumes with AI review and matching, and discovers European jobs via EURES/ESCO. This document is the roadmap for everything the rest of the tour shows in code.

tour.title: The Domain Data Model
tour.description: Before touching runtime code, read the Prisma schema to learn the vocabulary of the domain. It defines the core aggregates the README describes: Jobs and their statuses/notes/tags, Automations and their runs, the Profile/Resume tree, the CRM Person aggregate, and the supporting tables for notifications, webhooks, enrichment, and API keys. Every server action and UI page later in the tour ultimately reads or writes these tables.
tour.languageLesson: Prisma's SQLite provider stores rich value objects (emails, phones, social profiles) as JSON-encoded TEXT columns. The schema is the single source of truth; `prisma generate` derives a fully typed client consumed everywhere via the shared `src/lib/db.ts` singleton.

tour.title: Server Bootstrap — Where the Backend Comes Alive
tour.description: Next.js App Router has no single main() — `instrumentation.ts` is the server boot hook. On startup it launches the automation scheduler and the separate CRM cron, registers all domain-event consumers, and pre-warms the holiday cache. Understanding this file tells you which background subsystems exist and how the otherwise event-driven app gets kicked into motion.
tour.languageLesson: Next.js calls the exported `register()` in `instrumentation.ts` once per server process. Long-running work (cache pre-warm) is deferred with `setImmediate` so it never blocks request handling.

tour.title: The Connector / Module ACL Architecture
tour.description: JobSync's central architectural idea is the App ↔ Connector ↔ Module Anti-Corruption Layer. `register-all.ts` is the integration entry point: a single side-effect import that self-registers every external Module. `manifest.ts` defines how each Module describes its identity, credentials, health, and resilience, and `registry.ts` is the one unified registry the whole app queries — no hardcoded knowledge of any specific provider.
tour.languageLesson: Self-registration via side-effect imports (`import "./modules/x"` triggers `moduleRegistry.register(...)` at the bottom of each module) keeps the registry open for extension and free of central arrays — adding a module never edits shared code.

tour.title: A Concrete Module — EURES Job Discovery
tour.description: Building on the manifest/registry abstractions from Step 4, the EURES module is a representative implementation of a `DataSourceConnector`. It translates the foreign EURES API into the shared `DiscoveredVacancy` domain type, declares its filter fields and specialized widgets in a manifest, and lists ESCO and Eurostat as health dependencies. This is the pattern every job board (Arbeitsagentur, JSearch, future StepStone/Indeed) follows.

tour.title: The Vacancy Pipeline — The Runner
tour.description: The Runner orchestrates a full automation run: it pushes resolved credentials into the active Module, searches for vacancies, deduplicates and blacklist-filters them, triggers best-effort enrichment, AI-matches them against the user's resume, and persists the results. This is where the discovery Modules from Step 5 actually produce the staged vacancies a user later swipes through.
tour.languageLesson: Enrichment is best-effort and non-blocking: the orchestrator runs a per-dimension fallback chain (first success wins) with per-module `Promise.race` timeouts, and never surfaces errors to the user — failures fall back to placeholders.

tour.title: Run Coordination & Scheduling
tour.description: Every automation run — scheduled or manual — must flow through the RunCoordinator, which uses an in-memory mutex per automation to prevent double-execution and reports live progress. The scheduler's cron loop drives it on a timer. This is the safety gate that sits in front of the Runner from Step 6.
tour.languageLesson: The coordinator is a `globalThis` singleton so it survives Next.js Hot Module Replacement in development — a pattern reused for the event bus and other long-lived in-process state.

tour.title: Server Actions as DDD Repositories
tour.description: JobSync uses Next.js server actions as Repository implementations, one file per aggregate. `job.actions.ts` is the Job aggregate's repository (job + notes + tags + status), `person.actions.ts` the CRM Person aggregate. Both return the typed `ActionResult<T>` value object and the `job.model.ts` domain types define the ubiquitous language. Note the IDOR rule visible throughout: every query is scoped by userId.

tour.title: The Domain Event Bus
tour.description: Subsystems stay decoupled by publishing typed domain events rather than calling each other directly. The TypedEventBus carries events like CompanyCreated, VacancyPromoted, and AutomationDegraded; consumers (enrichment trigger, CRM activity logger, notification dispatcher) subscribe and react. This is the glue that connects the pipeline of Steps 6–8 to enrichment and notifications.
tour.languageLesson: Events are created via a `createEvent()` typed factory, and every consumer revalidates payloads at runtime with Zod (`safeParsePayload`) — TypeScript types are erased at runtime, so the schema is the real boundary guard.

tour.title: Multi-Channel Notifications
tour.description: When events demand user-facing alerts, the ChannelRouter fans them out to every enabled channel — in-app, webhook, and email — each implementing the same NotificationChannel interface. A single per-dispatch DispatchContext supplies all user-scoped data so channels never query the DB for context. This shows how the event bus from Step 9 ultimately reaches the user.

tour.title: The UI — Dashboard & Staging Deck
tour.description: The React shell (`layout.tsx`) wraps the authenticated dashboard. The signature feature is the staging deck: a Tinder-style swipe UI where users triage AI-matched vacancies produced by the Runner. The StagingContainer/DeckView pair routes every action (swipe, button, keyboard, detail sheet) through one state machine, and MatchScoreRing visualizes the match scores computed back in Step 6.

tour.title: API Surface — Internal Routes & Public API v1
tour.description: Two namespaces coexist: `/api/*` serves the frontend and proxies EU APIs (always auth-gated), while `/api/v1/*` is the versioned public Open Host Service secured with API keys. Every v1 handler is wrapped by `withApiAuth()` (CORS + auth + rate limit + error sanitization) and uses explicit Prisma `select` shapes so internal fields never leak.

tour.title: Internationalization — The Adapter Pattern
tour.description: Every user-visible string is translated across four locales (EN/DE/FR/ES). The i18n system hides its dictionary implementation behind two adapter barrels: `@/i18n` for client components and the `server-only` `@/i18n/server` for actions and routes. Consumers never import internals, so the backend could swap to LinguiJS later without touching call sites — `src/i18n/index.ts` is the single most depended-upon module in the codebase.
tour.languageLesson: The `import "server-only"` marker in the server barrel makes the build fail if a client component ever imports server translation code — a compile-time enforcement of the client/server boundary.

tour.title: Specs & Tests — The Safety Net
tour.description: Finally, see how behavior is locked in. Allium specs (`module-lifecycle`, `scheduler-coordination`) are the authoritative source of truth for domain rules written before implementation. Jest covers units and components, while Playwright E2E suites — guided by `e2e/CONVENTIONS.md` — exercise critical paths like the automation wizard and CRUD flows end to end.
